query: best prophylaxis for dental procedures in patients with mitral valve disease

retrieved_docs.title: Mitral Valve Prolapse -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: MVP patients with no symptoms often require no treatment. [12] [13] [14] MVP patients with symptoms of dysautonomia (chest pain, palpitations) should be treated with beta-blockers such as propranolol. MVP with severe mitral regurgitation may benefit from mitral valve repair or mitral valve replacement. ACC/AHA guidelines recommend mitral valve repair before symptoms of congestive heart failure develop. Individuals with MVP are at high risk for bacterial endocarditis. Until 2007, the American Heart Association (AHA) recommended prescribing antibiotics before invasive procedures, including dental surgery. Newer AHA guidelines recommend prophylaxis for dental procedures only should be advised for patients who have other cardiac conditions, which put them at the highest risk of adverse outcomes from infective endocarditis.

retrieved_docs.title: Mitral Valve Prolapse -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The management of MVP is with an interprofessional team that includes a team of a cardiologist, cardiac nurse, primary care provider, and cardiac surgeon. Patients need to be educated that they have a benign disorder and the risk of complications is low. However, patients need to be told about mitral regurgitation and endocarditis symptoms and when to present to the hospital. Clinicians should educate patients that they should adopt a healthy lifestyle, not smoke, exercise regularly, and abstain from alcohol and caffeinated beverages. For those who develop palpitations, a trial of beta-blockers may prove to be useful. [16] [17] [18] These patients also need to be educated that, in most cases, antibiotic prophylaxis is not required before a dental procedure. The cardiac nurse should obtain an ECG if the patient complains of palpitations, as 1 of the reason may be atrial arrhythmias. Follow-up is necessary as some patients may develop worsening mitral insufficiency and intolerable symptoms. These patients are best referred to a cardiac thoracic surgeon.

retrieved_docs.title: Mitral Stenosis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Endocarditis prophylaxis should only be given to high-risk patients before dental procedures involving gingival tissue manipulation or perforation of the oral mucosa. High-risk patients are those patients with a prosthetic heart valve or prosthetic material used for valve repair, previous history of infective endocarditis, and cardiac valvuloplasty. Rheumatic fever prophylaxis with Benzathine penicillin is the primary prevention treatment in patients with streptococcal pharyngitis.

retrieved_docs.title: Mitral Stenosis (Nursing) -- Health Teaching and Health Promotion
retrieved_docs.content: Educate patient on the disease process Discuss with the patient the importance of immunization Educate patients on prophylaxis to prevent endocarditis during dental procedures. Discuss plan of care with patient

retrieved_docs.title: Mitral Stenosis (Nursing) -- Medical Management
retrieved_docs.content: Endocarditis prophylaxis should only be given to high-risk patients before dental procedures involving gingival tissue manipulation or perforation of the oral mucosa. High-risk patients are those patients with a prosthetic heart valve or prosthetic material used for valve repair, previous history of infective endocarditis, and cardiac valvuloplasty. Rheumatic fever prophylaxis with Benzathine penicillin is the primary prevention treatment in patients with streptococcal pharyngitis.

retrieved_docs.title: Tricuspid Regurgitation -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Dental care: Patients with heart valve disease should maintain good oral hygiene and visit the dentist every 3 months. Antibiotics may be necessary before dental procedures to prevent endocarditis. Strep throat: Individuals with heart valve disease must seek medical attention promptly if they suspect they have strep throat. Atrial fibrillation: People with tricuspid regurgitation may develop an irregular heartbeat, which increases the risk of blood clot formation.

retrieved_docs.title: Antibiotic Prophylaxis in Dental and Oral Surgery Practice -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Infective endocarditis is the infection of the innermost layer of the heart, known as the endocardium. It typically affects the cardiac or prosthetic valves and cardiac devices. [18] To date, there has not been a randomized controlled trial regarding antimicrobial prophylaxis before dental procedures to inform clinical decision-making. However, several studies have evaluated the incidence of infective endocarditis before and after changes in treatment guidelines from prominent medical organizations. In other words, much of the data informing on this topic are epidemiological studies before and after changes in recommendations. Many epidemiological studies before and after the publication of antimicrobial prophylaxis recommendations have shown no change. A study in Olmstead County, Minnesota, in 2007 evaluated the incidence of infective endocarditis caused by viridans group streptococci before and after the change in the joint American Heart Association/American Dental Association guidelines, which narrowed recommendations for antimicrobial prophylaxis before dental procedures and did not reveal any change in the incidence of infective endocarditis. [19] Likewise, a study in France did not reveal an increased incidence of the condition after recommendations for antimicrobial prophylaxis were narrowed in 2002. [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Mitral Regurgitation -- Postoperative and Rehabilitation Care
retrieved_docs.content: Most patients can be discharged postoperatively if no complications (ie, infection or bleeding) arise during the procedure or hospitalization. Postoperative guidelines have undergone study, and the following are the recommendations: A postoperative ECG should be performed soon after the procedure before discharge and is periodically necessary to assess left ventricular function, especially if symptoms arise. Lifelong anticoagulation, typically with warfarin, is indicated for mechanical or prosthetic valve replacements. Clinicians should initiate antibiotic prophylaxis for infective endocarditis before dental, oral, or upper respiratory tract procedures in patients with a mechanical or prosthetic valve or a history of endocarditis. [27] [62] [63]

retrieved_docs.title: Antibiotic Prophylaxis in Dental and Oral Surgery Practice -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: A prosthetic valve or implanted prosthetic cardiac material is present. Materials include prosthetic valves in place, prosthetic material from a prior cardiac valve repair, or other prosthetic material in situ, including a transvenous aortic valve replacement. It also includes patients with a left ventricular assist device or artificial heart. [40] A previous episode of infective endocarditis has occurred. [40] Certain patients with congenital heart disease meet specific conditions: those with unrepaired cyanotic congenital heart defects, repaired congenital defects with prosthetic material within 6 months of repair, repaired congenital heart disease with a retained prosthetic device and residual defect, and patients with a pulmonary artery valve or conduit in place. [40] Cardiac transplant patients with valvulopathy. [40]

retrieved_docs.title: Antibiotic Prophylaxis in Dental and Oral Surgery Practice -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: However, some studies did reveal increased rates of endocarditis after the publication of guideline changes. Another US study revealed that the rate of streptococcal infective endocarditis has increased since the 2007 joint American Heart Association/American Dental Association guidelines, but did not note an increase in valvular replacement. [21] A study conducted on the incidence of infective endocarditis in England revealed an increase in the incidence of infective endocarditis after the guidelines in 2008 by the National Institute for Health and Clinical Excellence, which did not recommend antimicrobial prophylaxis in any population. [22] These guidelines were updated in 2016 to indicate that the organization does not generally recommend antimicrobial prophylaxis before dental procedures. [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Mitral Valve Prolapse -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Patients diagnosed with MVP should be advised to seek medical advice if the symptoms worsen. They have more benefits in surgical treatment before they develop congestive heart failure.

retrieved_docs.title: ACC/AHA Guidelines for Valve Disease -- Summary / Explanation -- Prosthetic Valves
retrieved_docs.content: The decision to use a mechanical versus a bioprosthetic surgical valve should involve shared decision-making and consider the patient's age, life expectancy, risk of long-term oral anticoagulation, risk of reintervention, anatomical considerations, access to medical care, and patient preference. For patients with mechanical valves undergoing minor procedures (eg, dental extraction, cataract surgery), vitamin K antagonist anticoagulation should be continued. For patients with bileaflet mechanical valves and no other thromboembolism risk factor undergoing invasive procedures, temporary interruption of the anticoagulation without bridging therapy is recommended. For patients with bioprosthetic heart valves and atrial fibrillation with a high CHA 2 DS 2 VASc score, patients with a mechanical mitral valve, and patients with a mechanical aortic valve with significant risk for thromboembolism, bridging therapy should be considered, when the INR is subtherapeutic. Increasing the INR goal or adding low-dose aspirin is reasonable in patients with a mechanical heart valve and a systemic embolic event on therapeutic INR.

retrieved_docs.title: Subacute Bacterial Endocarditis Prophylaxis -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Preventive measures against infective endocarditis include the following steps: Maintaining optimal oral hygiene. Administering antibiotics prophylactically before certain invasive dental or oral procedures. Diagnosing and treating endocarditis infections promptly. Utilizing antibiotic prophylaxis for surgical site infections to mitigate the risk of subsequent endocarditis. For instance, administering antibiotic prophylaxis before cardiac surgery serves as one example.

retrieved_docs.title: Infectious Endocarditis -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Although antibiotic prophylaxis remains controversial, the AHA/ACC continues to recommend certain individuals undergoing high-risk procedures receive pharmacological prophylaxis. As detailed in the 2017 AHA/ACC focused update, patients with prosthetic cardiac valves, prosthetic material used for valve repair, previous infective endocarditis, unrepaired cyanotic congenital heart disease, repaired congenital heart disease with persistent valvular insufficiency, or cardiac transplants with structurally dysfunctional valves should be considered for antibiotic prophylaxis before any dental procedures requiring mucosal perforation or manipulation of gingival or periapical tissue. [23] A potential prophylactic regimen would include 2 grams of amoxicillin or 600 milligrams of clindamycin (for those with a contraindication to a beta-lactam) less than 60 minutes from procedure start time. [20] Note that current guidelines no longer recommend antibiotic prophylaxis for any cutaneous, genitourinary, and gastrointestinal procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Unerupted Teeth -- Contraindications -- Cardiovascular Diseases
retrieved_docs.content: Patients who have a high risk of developing infective endocarditis or have had it in the past may need antibiotic prophylaxis. Consultation with a cardiologist or primary care provider is recommended. [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Endocarditis -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: In 2007, the AHA modified their infective endocarditis prophylaxis guidelines, and the indications for prophylaxis were reduced for dental procedures, genitourinary, and gastrointestinal tract procedures. [8] [16] Indications for prophylaxis based on 2007 AHA: Prosthetic cardiac valve or prosthetic material used for cardiac valve repair Previous infective endocarditis Cardiac Heart Disease (CHD) with: Unrepaired cyanotic CHD (including shunts and conduits) CHD entirely repaired with prosthetic material or device, during the first 6 months after the procedure Repaired CHD with residual defects to the site of a prosthetic patch or prosthetic device Cardiac transplantation recipients who develop cardiac valvulopathy The particular individual condition of these recommendations makes reasonable direct consultation with pediatric cardiology to determine the ongoing need for prophylaxis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1884
retrieved_docs.content: Mitral valve prolapse (تدلي الصمام المترالي) is classified as a form of valvular heart disease (مرض الصمام القلبي), indicating that this condition involves abnormal movement of the mitral valve leaflets and falls within the broader spectrum of diseases affecting cardiac valves. Recognizing mitral valve prolapse as a type of valvular heart disease is essential for accurate diagnosis, risk assessment, and appropriate management of patients with cardiac valve abnormalities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1723

Recognizing this link helps clinicians consider valvular assessment in individuals with systemic disorders, facilitating earlier diagnosis and more comprehensive management of potential cardiac involvement.

Recognizing that these conditions fall under the broader category of heart valve disease is important for accurate diagnosis, risk assessment, and the selection of appropriate therapeutic strategies in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 4 -- Community 2846
retrieved_docs.content: The concept of an allowed qualifier is applied to the prevention and control of mitral valve stenosis, threatened abortion, and fetal distress syndrome. Preventive and control strategies for mitral valve stenosis aim to limit valve narrowing and reduce cardiac complications. In cases of threatened abortion, early identification and appropriate interventions are essential to prevent progression to miscarriage and to protect maternal and fetal health. For fetal distress syndrome, vigilant monitoring and timely therapeutic measures are crucial to manage fetal compromise and improve outcomes for both mother and child. Integrating prevention and control measures for these conditions supports better patient care and reduces the risk of adverse events.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 5 -- Community 2948
retrieved_docs.content: Allowed qualifier is identified as both a complication and a target of care for several distinct medical conditions. It is linked to the complication of iron‑deficiency anemia (فقر الدم الناتج عن نقص الحديد) and also requires appropriate management for this anemia. In addition, adhesion (الالتصاق) is noted as a complication associated with allowed qualifier and is likewise an area that demands specific therapeutic attention. Tongue disorder (اِضطِراب اللسان) is another complication connected to allowed qualifier, and appropriate care measures are indicated for this condition. Finally, mitral valve stenosis (تضيُّق الصمام المترالي) is listed as a complication of allowed qualifier, with corresponding treatment considerations required to address this cardiac narrowing. Together, these relationships emphasize that allowed qualifier encompasses a range of complications—iron‑deficiency anemia, adhesion, tongue disorder, and mitral valve stenosis—and that each of these conditions necessitates dedicated clinical management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2613
retrieved_docs.content: Failure (biologic function) is linked to both Rheumatic mitral valve failure and الفَشَلٌ الكَبِدِيُّ الكُلْوِيّ. Rheumatic mitral valve failure refers to damage of the mitral valve caused by rheumatic heart disease, leading to impaired cardiac function. الفَشَلٌ الكَبِدِيُّ الكُلْوِيّ denotes a combined hepatic‑renal failure, a severe condition in which both liver and kidney functions are compromised. The association of general biologic function failure with these distinct organ‑specific failures underscores the interconnected nature of systemic physiological decline, where dysfunction in one system may reflect or contribute to serious cardiac valvular disease as well as simultaneous liver and kidney impairment. Recognizing these relationships can aid clinicians in comprehensive assessment and integrated management of patients presenting with multi‑organ failure.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2478
retrieved_docs.content: Failure (biologic function) is a central concept that links specific organ dysfunctions and overall patient assessment. It is associated with rheumatic mitral valve failure, indicating that impairment of the biological function can manifest as damage to the mitral valve caused by rheumatic disease. It is also associated with الفَشَلٌ الكَبِدِيُّ الكُلْوِيّ, reflecting a combined hepatic‑renal failure in which both liver and kidney functions are compromised. In clinical practice, the presence and extent of such biologic failure are used to interpret the general clinical state, providing clinicians with insight into the patient’s overall health status and guiding comprehensive management strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 2513
retrieved_docs.content: Rheumatic heart valve stenosis and rheumatic mitral valve obstruction are specific rheumatic conditions that affect the cardiac valves. Both entities are classified within the broader category of heart valve disorders, indicating that they represent pathological processes involving valve function. In addition, rheumatic mitral valve obstruction is identified as a structural disorder of the heart, reflecting that it entails anatomical alteration of the mitral valve apparatus. Together, these relationships illustrate that rheumatic valve disease can present as stenosis or obstruction, and that such manifestations are encompassed by the general concepts of heart valve disorders and structural heart disease, which is important for accurate diagnosis, classification, and management in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 2 -- Community 1072
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiac valvotomy is classified as a type of عملية صمام القلب (heart valve operation) and is specifically linked to استئصال تأموري (valve excision). Within the broader category of عمليات صمام القلب, several more narrowly defined procedures are recognized, including إعاضة الصمام الثلاثي الشرف (tricuspid valve replacement), إعاضة الصمام الرئوي (pulmonary valve replacement), استبدال الصمام الأبهري (aortic valve replacement), and استبدال الصمام التاجي (mitral valve replacement). These relationships illustrate that cardiac valvotomy and valve excision are part of the general spectrum of heart valve surgeries, while the named replacement procedures represent distinct, focused interventions targeting individual cardiac valves. Understanding how these procedures are organized under the umbrella of heart valve operations aids clinicians in selecting appropriate surgical strategies and communicating procedural options to patients.
